items.title: The impact of cutting USAID on America's soft power: How China looks to react | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Kennedy created the US Agency for International Development, USAID, in 1961 to be an umbrella organization for all US nonmilitary foreign aid. At the time, aid to contain communism was the Cold War guiding principle, but not the only one.

Kennedy also invoked a moral responsibilty, pointing to America's economic obligations as the wealthiest people in a world of largely poor people. Six decades later USAID provides tens of billions of dollars of humanitarian aid to countries around the world.

But that is about to change. In a move to slash wasteful spending, spearheaded by Elon Musk in the Department of Government Efficiency, the Trump White House this week began slashing most of USAID's workforce. It's feared that the decision to defund USAID by Donald Trump and Elon Musk, two of the world's richest people could hurt millions of the world's poorest people.

Chapters:
0:00 The history of USAID
4:27 Rorry Daniels, Asia Society Policy Institute
10:33 Hardin Lang, Refugees International

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

For more news, analysis and background information on the German election 2025 go to our dedicated page: https://www.dw.com/en/german-election-2025/t-70794502

items.title: Russia takes the mining town Toretsk, as its troops close on the Ukrainian city of Pokrovsk| DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russia says it has captured the key mining town of Toretsk in eastern Ukraine after months of fighting.
Ukraine has already lost the output from a crucial coal mine in the area ‚Äď the only one producing coking coal for its steel industry. Operations were suspended last month because of the Russian advance. Moscow's troops are also closing in on Pokrovsk -- a key logistics hub.  Its main supply lines are now under threat. 

00:00 Russia says it has taken the town of Toretsk, in eastern Ukraine
00:38 DW's Rebecca Ritters reports from the area, where locals face a life or death question - to stay or to leave. 


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Is there a crack in Germany‚Äôs political firewall against the far-right? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany‚Äôs likely next Chancellor Friedrich Merz just turned the election campaign on its head. He took a major political risk by relying on the far right AfD party to deliver a majority in parliament for the first time. Tens of thousands of people took to the streets to protest against any cooperation with the far right. Did Merz just throw away his chance of a smooth ride to become Germany‚Äôs next chancellor? Or is he about to break the political paralysis around the AfD‚Äôs lifeblood issue: Migration.  

DW Chief Political Editor Michaela K√ľfner, talks to journalist and author Justus Bender and Matthew Moore, DW's  Political Correspondent. 

00:00 Introduction 
1:28 Merz‚Äô way or no way  
2:22 Why vote with the AfD?  
3:05 Static polls?  
6:12 Broken promise?  
9:40 Merkel not amused  
12:16 Merz vs Merkel 
15:17 Merz‚Äė pledge 
17:46 AfD in power ‚Äď really?! 
19:35 German stability? 
21:30 ‚ÄúWorst case‚ÄĚ is now 
25:23 Coalition options 
28:18 AfD in luck  

LINKS

Merz under Fire  German opposition leader Merz under fire after joint vote with AfD reaches majority | DW News

items.author: DW News
items.content:

Ukraine is believed to be rich in valuable 'rare earth' metals that are needed for manufacturing batteries, smart phones, electric vehicles - and weapons.

items.title: How will Trump‚Äôs sanctions affect the work of the International Criminal Court? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>International condemnation is growing louder after US President Donald Trump's executive order imposing sanctions on the International Criminal Court.  German Chancellor Olaf Scholz said the move jeopardizes an institution which was set up to make sure dictators can't persecute people and start wars. The United Nations is calling on Trump to reverse his decision.

items.title: Cooperation with the far right: The impact on the polls | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Two issues have dominated the German elections in recent weeks: migration and questions about whether the centre-right CDU would work with the far-right AfD. The head of the CDU, Friedrich Merz has ruled that out. But he's under pressure after he accepted votes from the AfD to pass a motion. That broke a taboo in German politics and it could hurt Merz's chances of winning the election. 

#right #germany #elections

items.title: What is the situation on the popular Greek island? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Greek authorities have declared a state of emergency, after the most powerful earthquake so far shook the island of Santorini. An unprecedented wave of tremors has forced nearly all residents and tourists to leave and officials are warning of a high risk of landslides. Santorini sits on top of a volcano. The last major eruption was in 1950. But experts say the recent tremors are not related to volcanic activity but to tectonic plate movements.

#santorini #earthquake #greece

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>We are in the middle of a revolution. AI can now do things we didn't dream possible just a few years, or even months ago. As China's DeepSeek shows, innovation is getting cheaper - and more accessible. Meanwhile, Alphabet, one of the world's most powerful tech companies, says it's now willing to develop AI weapons. items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.author: DW News
items.content:

That infuriated student groups and other activists, who proceeded to tear down and vandalize buildings linked to Hasina.

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany is taking part in a new NATO operation aimed at increasing surveillance and showing a stronger presence in the Baltic Sea. It follows increasing incidents of damage to undersea cables and infrastructure.

items.title: Can mining change the Taliban‚Äôs fortune? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Afghanistan has been facing decades of economic crisis which has been compounded by the Taliban's return to power. One of the ways the country has been trying to increase revenues is by mining its gemstone and mineral resources.
DW reports from one mine in Goshta in eastern Afghanistan where jade extraction is helping the local economy.

The United Nations has condemned the recent freeze on US aid programs in Afghanistan, saying that it will suspend services that "provide a lifeline for women and girls in crisis" living under Taliban rule. Afghanistan is one of the deadliest countries in the world for women to give birth. The UN says the slash in US support will result in around 1,200 maternal deaths and more than a hundred thousand unintended pregnancies in the next three years. 

00:00 Mining on rise as Afghanistan seeks financial boost
02:53 Beenish Javed, DW Reporter
06:14 US suspends aid for Taliban-ruled Afghanistan
07:31 Amin Saikal, Australian National University


#afghanistan #taliban #trump 
For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why the car industry won't escape Trump's tariff reprieve | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The car industry is on edge. Trump's threatened tariffs could see a levy of up to 25% slapped on imports,  potentially adding $3000 onto the average price of a new car. As the world's second biggest car market and one that relies heavily on imported parts, the consequences within the United States could be significant. So how worried should carmakers and consumers be? DW Business speaks with Gregor Sebastian, Senior Analyst at Rhodium Group and Joe McCabe, CEO at Auto Forecast Solutions.

items.title: Trump faces international backlash over Gaza remarks | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump has announced that his country will 'take over' the Gaza Strip and that Palestinians living there could be resettled elsewhere.  He made the comments at a White House press conference alongside Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. US allies across Europe and the Middle East have condemned Trump's plans.

items.title: Protesters in Serbia put pressure on President Vucic | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Student-led demonstrations in Serbia show no sign of letting up, three months after 15 people were killed when a train station canopy collapsed in the country's second largest city. Students blame government corruption and incompetence for the disaster in Novi Sad, and have taken their protest nationwide. The main target of their anger is president Aleksandar Vucic, who's facing the biggest test of his 12-year rule.

0:00 Protests in Serbia are still going strong

items.title: Concerns grow over Musk's role in federal spending decisions | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>There is growing concern in the US over Elon Musk's role in federal spending decisions. US President Trump has empowered Musk and his team to make drastic cuts. In the last two weeks, he's shuttered several government programs and moved to close the US Agency for International Development. Most recently he's gained access to closely held financial systems - including the US Treasury Department's payment system. Democrats say the situation is dangerous, unconstitutional and a power grab. 

0:00 Growing concern over Elon Musk's decisions
2:44 Richard H. Pildes, Professor of Constitututional Law, NYU

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The government in the Democratic Republic of Congo says the unilateral ceasefire declared by Rwanda-backed rebels fighting in the east of the country is "false communication." The rebels took the city of Goma last week. The United Nations says at least 900 people have been killed in the fighting.

items.title: Why has Syria's new leader al-Sharaa pledged to work with Turkey's Erdogan? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Turkey's president Recep Tayyip Erdogan has praised Syria's interim leader, Ahmed al-Sharaa for his strong commitment to fighting terror. Mr al-Sharaa visited Ankara to seek support to rebuild Syria after 13 years of civil war. President Erdogan has repeatedly signaled his willingness to support Syria, despite Turkey's current economic woes. In return, Ankara wants support from Damascus in its efforts to disband the Kurdish militant-run Syrian Democratic Forces in northeastern Syria.  

DW speaks to Guido Steinberg, North Africa and Middle East analyst at the German Institute for International Security Affairs

Chapters:
0:00 Erdogan praises Syrian leader
0:44 Guido Steinberg, German Institute for International & Security Affairs

items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.title: What role does USAID play in US foreign policy, and why are Trump and Musk slashing its funding?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The US Agency for International Development (USAID) has announced that all its direct hire personnel will be put on administrative leave worldwide, as President Donald Trump's orders to merge the agency with the State Department come into force. The move raises questions about the future of various programs around the world. America has until now been the biggest single international donor. But the change in the White House, and the apparently growing influence of Elon Musk on spending decisions,  has transformed the picture.

DW speaks to former State Department official Tyson Barker about USAID's role in supporting Ukraine and other foreign policy initiatives. 

Chapters:
0:00 Tyson Barker, Former state department official

items.title: Is Trump's tariff pause on Mexico aimed at renegotiating his US-Mexico-Canada trade deal? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>China has announced that it is going to impose tariffs on US imports, including oil and coal, in response to levies against Chinese imports imposed by US President Donald Trump, which have now come into force.
